--- SOURCE 1: Plasmonic nanoparticles - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles

SUMMARY:
Plasmonic nanoparticles are particles whose electron density can couple with electromagnetic radiation of wavelengths that are far larger than the particle due to the nature of the dielectric-metal interface between the medium and the particles: unlike in a pure metal where there is a maximum limit on what size wavelength can be effectively coupled based on the material size.[2] What differentiates these particles from normal surface plasmons is that plasmonic nanoparticles also exhibit interesting scattering, absorbance, and coupling properties based on their geometries and relative positions.[3][4] These unique properties have made them a focus of research in many applications including solar cells, spectroscopy, signal enhancement for imaging, and cancer treatment.[5][6] Their high sensitivity also identifies them as good candidates for designing mechano-optical instrumentation.[7]

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

[](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Time_evolution_of_a_plane-wave_pulse_interacting_with_the_nanoparticles_(lateral).ogv "Play video")Duration: 17 seconds.0:17[](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Time_evolution_of_a_plane-wave_pulse_interacting_with_the_nanoparticles_(lateral).ogv)

[FDTD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Finite-difference_time-domain_method "Finite-difference time-domain method") simulation of a pulsed [plane wave](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plane_wave "Plane wave") interaction with plasmonic nanoparticles[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-1)

**Plasmonic nanoparticles** are particles whose electron density can [couple](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coupling_(physics) "Coupling (physics)") with [electromagnetic radiation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation") of wavelengths that are far larger than the particle due to the nature of the [dielectric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dielectric "Dielectric")-[metal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metal "Metal") interface between the medium and the particles: unlike in a pure metal where there is a maximum limit on what size wavelength can be effectively coupled based on the material size.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-2)

What differentiates these particles from normal [surface plasmons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surface_plasmons "Surface plasmons") is that plasmonic nanoparticles also exhibit interesting [scattering](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scattering "Scattering"), [absorbance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Absorbance "Absorbance"), and [coupling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmon_Coupling "Plasmon Coupling") properties based on their geometries and relative positions.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-3)[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-4) These unique properties have made them a focus of research in many applications including solar cells, spectroscopy, signal enhancement for imaging, and cancer treatment.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-:0-5)[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-6) Their high sensitivity also identifies them as good candidates for designing mechano-optical instrumentation.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-7)

[Plasmons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmons "Plasmons") are the oscillations of free electrons that are the consequence of the formation of a [dipole](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dipole "Dipole") in the material due to electromagnetic waves. The electrons migrate in the material to restore its initial state; however, the light waves oscillate, leading to a constant shift in the dipole that forces the electrons to oscillate at the same frequency as the light. This coupling only occurs when the frequency of the light is equal to or less than the plasma frequency and is greatest at the plasma frequency that is therefore called the [resonant frequency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Resonant_frequency "Resonant frequency"). The scattering and absorbance cross-sections describe the intensity of a given frequency to be scattered or absorbed. Many fabrication processes or chemical synthesis methods exist for preparation of such nanoparticles, depending on the desired size and geometry.

The nanoparticles can form clusters (the so-called "plasmonic molecules") and interact with each other to form cluster states. The symmetry of the nanoparticles and the distribution of the electrons within them can affect a type of bonding or antibonding character between the nanoparticles similarly to molecular orbitals. Since light couples with the electrons, polarized light can be used to control the distribution of the electrons and alter the mulliken term symbol for the irreducible representation. [Changing the geometry of the nanoparticles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shape_control_in_nanocrystal_growth "Shape control in nanocrystal growth") can be used to manipulate the optical activity and properties of the system, but so can the polarized light by lowering the symmetry of the conductive electrons inside the particles and changing the dipole moment of the cluster. These clusters can be used to manipulate light on the nano scale.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-8)

Theory
------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Plasmonic_nanoparticles&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Theory")]

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/99/Question_book-new.svg/60px-Question_book-new.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg)This section **needs additional citations for [verification](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:EditPage/Plasmonic_nanoparticles "Special:EditPage/Plasmonic nanoparticles") by [adding citations to reliable sources](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed._(September 2013)_ _([Learn how and when to remove this message](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Maintenance\_template\_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))_

The quasistatic equations that describe the [scattering](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scattering "Scattering") and [absorbance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Absorbance "Absorbance") cross-sections for very small spherical nanoparticles are:

σ s c a t t=8 π 3 k 4 R 6|ε p a r t i c l e−ε m e d i u m ε p a r t i c l e+2 ε m e d i u m|2{\displaystyle {{\sigma }_{\rm {scatt}}}={\frac {8\pi }{3}}{{k}^{4}}{{R}^{6}}{{\left|{\frac {{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {particle}}}-{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {medium}}}}{{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {particle}}}+2{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {medium}}}}}\right|}^{2}}}![Image 5: {\displaystyle {{\sigma }_{\rm {scatt}}}={\frac {8\pi }{3}}{{k}^{4}}{{R}^{6}}{{\left|{\frac {{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {particle}}}-{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {medium}}}}{{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {particle}}}+2{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {medium}}}}}\right|}^{2}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7799b54980c75aa3ab2454a91a72e1ea2194b86c)

σ a b s=4 π k R 3 Im⁡|ε p a r t i c l e−ε m e d i u m ε p a r t i c l e+2 ε m e d i u m|{\displaystyle {{\sigma }_{\rm {abs}}}=4\pi k{{R}^{3}}\operatorname {Im} \left|{\frac {{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {particle}}}-{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {medium}}}}{{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {particle}}}+2{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {medium}}}}}\right|}![Image 6: {\displaystyle {{\sigma }_{\rm {abs}}}=4\pi k{{R}^{3}}\operatorname {Im} \left|{\frac {{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {particle}}}-{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {medium}}}}{{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {particle}}}+2{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {medium}}}}}\right|}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a83978550d1f08a70786a519f4bb24efc4e5c9a9)

where k{\displaystyle k}![Image 7: {\displaystyle k}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c3c9a2c7b599b37105512c5d570edc034056dd40) is the [wavenumber](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wavenumber "Wavenumber") of the electric field, R{\displaystyle R}![Image 8: {\displaystyle R}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4b0bfb3769bf24d80e15374dc37b0441e2616e33) is the radius of the particle, ε m e d i u m{\displaystyle {{\varepsilon }_{\rm {medium}}}}![Image 9: {\displaystyle {{\varepsilon }_{\rm {medium}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/03a65a5cba0429b72897f229f9638097c027c3d9) is the relative [permittivity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Permittivity "Permittivity") of the [dielectric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dielectric "Dielectric") medium and ε p a r t i c l e{\displaystyle {{\varepsilon }_{\rm {particle}}}}![Image 10: {\displaystyle {{\varepsilon }_{\rm {particle}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/416b68d59ed90a82c7b8718e0de5952b89e63ce6) is the [relative permittivity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Relative_permittivity "Relative permittivity") of the nanoparticle defined by

ε p a r t i c l e=1−ω p 2 ω 2+i ω γ{\displaystyle {{\varepsilon }_{\rm {particle}}}=1-{\frac {\omega _{\rm {p}}^{2}}{{{\omega }^{2}}+\mathrm {i} {\omega }{\gamma }}}}![Image 11: {\displaystyle {{\varepsilon }_{\rm {particle}}}=1-{\frac {\omega _{\rm {p}}^{2}}{{{\omega }^{2}}+\mathrm {i} {\omega }{\gamma }}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e5f7b6b55ad18dc9901650c82298555f2bd01c19)

also known as the [Drude Model](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Drude_Model "Drude Model") for free electrons where ω p{\displaystyle {{\omega }_{\rm {p}}}}![Image 12: {\displaystyle {{\omega }_{\rm {p}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/78ee8b566ba2adc391b0e23b8565116033153f63) is the [plasma frequency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasma_frequency "Plasma frequency"), γ{\displaystyle {\gamma }}![Image 13: {\displaystyle {\gamma }}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0c5a51bb731eb471125cf853d8c845695870617f) is the relaxation frequency of the charge carries, and ω{\displaystyle \omega }![Image 14: {\displaystyle \omega }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/48eff443f9de7a985bb94ca3bde20813ea737be8) is the frequency of the electromagnetic radiation. This equation is the result of solving the [differential equation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Differential_equation "Differential equation") for a [harmonic oscillator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harmonic_oscillator "Harmonic oscillator") with a driving force proportional to the electric field that the particle is subjected to. For a more thorough derivation, see [surface plasmon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surface_plasmon "Surface plasmon").

It logically follows that the [resonance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Resonance "Resonance") conditions for these equations is reached when the denominator is around zero such that

ε p a r t i c l e+2 ε m e d i u m≈0{\displaystyle {{\varepsilon }_{\rm {particle}}}+2{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {medium}}}\approx 0}![Image 15: {\displaystyle {{\varepsilon }_{\rm {particle}}}+2{{\varepsilon }_{\rm {medium}}}\approx 0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e73ac6d5b3d9cb2a25c08d7dd6c002132b9cc6a1)

When this condition is fulfilled the cross-sections are at their maximum.

These cross-sections are for single, spherical particles. The equations change when particles are non-spherical, or are coupled to 1 or more other nanoparticles, such as when their geometry changes. This principle is important for several applications.

Rigorous electrodynamic analysis of plasma oscillations in a spherical metal nanoparticle of a finite size was performed in.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-9)

Applications
------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Plasmonic_nanoparticles&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Applications")]

### Plasmonic solar cells

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Plasmonic_nanoparticles&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Plasmonic solar cells")]

Main article: [Plasmonic solar cell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_solar_cell "Plasmonic solar cell")

Due to their ability to scatter light back into the [photovoltaic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photovoltaic "Photovoltaic") structure and low absorption, plasmonic nanoparticles are under investigation as a method for increasing solar cell efficiency.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-10)[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-:0-5) Forcing more light to be absorbed by the dielectric increases efficiency.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-11)

Plasmons can be excited by optical radiation and induce an electric current from [hot electrons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hot-carrier_injection "Hot-carrier injection") in materials fabricated from gold particles and light-sensitive molecules of [porphin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Porphin "Porphin"), of precise sizes and specific patterns. The wavelength to which the plasmon responds is a function of the size and spacing of the particles. The material is fabricated using [ferroelectric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ferroelectric "Ferroelectric")[nanolithography](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nanolithography "Nanolithography"). Compared to conventional [photoexcitation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photoexcitation "Photoexcitation"), the material produced three to 10 times the current.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-12)[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-13)

### Spectroscopy

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Plasmonic_nanoparticles&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Spectroscopy")]

In the past 5 years plasmonic nanoparticles have been explored as a method for high resolution [spectroscopy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spectroscopy "Spectroscopy"). One group utilized 40 nm gold nanoparticles that had been functionalized such that they would bind specifically to epidermal growth factor receptors to determine the density of those receptors on a cell. This technique relies on the fact that the effective geometry of the particles change when they appear within one particle diameter (40 nm) of each other. Within that range, quantitative information on the [EGFR](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epidermal_growth_factor_receptor "Epidermal growth factor receptor") density in the [cell membrane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cell_membrane "Cell membrane") can be retrieved based on the shift in resonant frequency of the plasmonic particles.[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-14)

### Cancer treatment

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Plasmonic_nanoparticles&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Cancer treatment")]

Plasmonic nanoparticles have demonstrated a wide potential for the establishment of innovative cancer treatments.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-Cassano_4%E2%80%9316-15) Despite that, there are still not plasmonic [nanomaterials](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nanomaterials "Nanomaterials") employed in the clinical practice, because the associated metal persistence.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-Cassano_4%E2%80%9316-15) Preliminary research indicates that some nanomaterials, among which [gold nanorods](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gold_nanorods "Gold nanorods")[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-16) and ultrasmall-in-nano architectures,[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-17) can convert [IR](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Infrared "Infrared") laser light into localized heat, also in combination with other established [cancer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cancer "Cancer") treatments.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmonic_nanoparticles#cite_note-18)

# The Science of Plasmonics

Plasmonic nanoparticles - including gold, silver and platinum particles - are discrete metallic particles that have unique optical properties due to their size and shape, and are increasingly being incorporated into commercial products and technologies. These technologies, which span fields ranging from photovoltaics to biological and chemical sensors, take advantage of the extraordinary efficiency of plasmonic nanoparticles at absorbing and scattering light. Additionally, unlike most dyes and pigments, plasmonic nanoparticles have a color that depends on their size and shape and can be tuned to optimize performance for individual applications without changing the chemical composition of the material: for example, the solutions below contain gold or silver nanoparticles with controlled size and shape that tunes their optical response, resulting in vibrantly colored dispersions.

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/MG_2984-Edit_large.jpg?v=1492712937)

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/MG_2984-Edit_large.jpg?v=1492712937)

This module describes the origin of the surface plasmon resonance in metal nanoparticles and introduces the resulting optical properties for this interesting class of materials.

## Plasmonic Nanoparticles

Plasmonic nanoparticles are extremely strong absorbers and scatters of light and are used in lateral flow diagnostics, surface enhanced spectroscopy, labeling, and color changing sensors. By changing nanoparticle size, shape, and composition, the optical response can be tuned from the ultraviolet through the visible to the near-infrared regions of the electromagnetic spectrum. By shifting the absorption and scattering, the color of nanoparticle dispersions and films can also be tuned: for example, solutions of spherical gold nanoparticles are ruby red in color due to the strong scattering and absorption in the green region of the spectrum, while solutions of silver nanoparticles are yellow due to the plasmon resonance in the blue region of the spectrum (red and green light is unaffected).

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/products-spheres_116d55c7-1178-4c28-847e-2a44b2b1293a_large.jpg?v=1493073128)

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/products-spheres_116d55c7-1178-4c28-847e-2a44b2b1293a_large.jpg?v=1493073128)

In addition to metal nanoparticles, there have been recent examples of metal oxide nanoparticles doped with other metal atoms that show strong and tunable plasmon resonances. By changing the particle size, dopant and dopant concentration, plasmon resonances in the near-infrared (NIR) and short-wavelength infrared (SWIR) have been fabricated.

## What is a Surface Plasmon Resonance?

The remarkable optical properties of plasmonic materials occurs because the conduction electrons on the nanoparticle surface undergo a collective oscillation when excited by light at specific wavelengths (shown below). This oscillation, which is known as a ***surface plasmon resonance*** (SPR), results in the unusually strong scattering and absorption of light. When these resonances are excited, absorption and scattering intensities can be up to 40x higher than identically sized particles that are not plasmonic.

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/plasmon2_large.jpg?v=1492711626)

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/plasmon2_large.jpg?v=1492711626)

### The Physics of Plasmon Resonances

The basis for the effect is the plasmon resonance of the free electrons in the metal nanoparticle, which can be understood by studying the polarizability (the ease with which charges, such as the conduction electrons on the metal nanoparticle surface, undergo charge distribution and form partial dipoles). For a spherical nanoparticle, the quasi-static polarizability of the nanoparticle is given by

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/plasmonic-equation_large.png?v=1492711611)where *ε*1 is the wavelength dependent dielectric function of the nanoparticle and *ε*2 is the dielectric function of the medium which remains roughly constant regardless of wavelength. When the condition Re{*ε*1} = -2*ε*2 is satisfied, the particle is driven into resonance resulting in a strong increase in the absorption and/or scattering at that wavelength. The resonance condition depends on the wavelength dependent dielectric function of the nanoparticle as well as the dielectric function of the medium. Consequently, the nanoparticle optical properties are highly dependent on material composition, size, and the medium in which the particles are embedded. For example, increasing the aspect ratio of gold nanorods causes the plasmon resonance to shift from the visible into the NIR, as shown below.

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/plasmonic-equation_large.png?v=1492711611)

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/nanorods-full-spectrum_large.gif?v=1469683953)

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/nanorods-full-spectrum_large.gif?v=1469683953)

Nanoparticle optical properties are also sensitive to the proximity of other plasmonic materials. When two or more plasmonic nanoparticles are near each other (with edge-to-edge separations of one particle diameter or less) their surface plasmons couple as the conduction electrons on each particle surface collectively oscillate. This effect is similar to molecular orbital theory in that plasmon coupling results in the oscillating electrons assuming the lowest energy state, causing the plasmon resonance wavelength of the coupled particles to red-shift to longer wavelengths (lower energies). This coupling effect is responsible for the dramatic changes in the visible color of plasmonic nanoparticle solution when nanoparticles aggregate and also the basis of many different types of plasmonic nanoparticle sensors.

### Modeling the Optical Response of Plasmonic Nanoparticles

At nanoComposix, we use modeling to predict the optical properties of a nanoparticle of a given size and shape in order to target morphologies to fabricate particles that will resonate at a desired wavelengths (e.g. with a specific laser line), as well as for quality control purposes to ensure that our nanoparticles are highly monodisperse and have uniform shapes.

Modeling for spherical nanoparticles is typically performed using Mie theory. Mie theory is not actually a theory, but rather is the analytical solution of Maxwell’s equations for the scattering of light by a spherical particle. Mie theory can be used to calculate the scattering from spheres of any diameter at any wavelength of light. While classical Mie theory assumed that the sphere would be composed of a single material, more recently, Mie theory has been adapted to solve light scattering from layered spheres. At nanoComposix, we have written a custom Mie theory code to calculate the extinction, absorption, and scattering from metal and dielectric nano- and micro-particles. Our online [Mie Theory calculator](https://nanocomposix.com/pages/tools) allows you to choose nanoparticle size, composition, and the properties of the media surrounding the particles, and calculate the scattering and absorption spectrum.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Plasmonic nanostructures exhibit exceptional light absorption and scattering due to their unique surface plasmon resonance, enhancing their optical responses based on size, shape, and material composition.
These nanostructures find applications in fields such as photovoltaics, biosensing, spectroscopy, and cancer treatment, leveraging their ability to enhance light-matter interactions.
Recent research has led to improved techniques for fabricating plasmonic nanoparticles, enhancing their performance for applications in energy conversion and medical diagnostics.
Ongoing studies focus on engineering nanostructures for optimized functionality, paving the way for breakthroughs in nano-electronics, healthcare, and renewable energy technologies.
